analysis.note: Lexical form atas; pausal/unsandhied spelling normalized as ataḥ. In sandhi before eva it is printed ata.
Pronominal form saḥ, printed sa before the following consonant.
First constituent of the sandhi group na eva anyaḥ > naivānyo.
Second constituent of the sandhi group na eva anyaḥ > naivānyo; it strengthens the negation: ‘not at all / not precisely’.
Predicate adjective agreeing with saḥ; its final -aḥ is printed -o before the following na.
First constituent of na ananyaḥ > nānanyaḥ.
Predicate adjective agreeing with saḥ: ‘not other, non-different’.

verification.detail: compound stem ananya as Pum/Prathama/Eka: pass; relation tatpurusa: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Ablatival tasil-type adverbial form, ‘from/than the dependent’, not the regular ablative singular paratantrāt.

verification.detail: avyayībhāva; whole form not in kosha/MW as indeclinable

analysis.note: Indeclinable with vati/-vat in the sense ‘like’; final -t is printed -d before vācyaḥ. The base is anityatādi, ‘impermanence and so on’.

verification.detail: avyayībhāva; whole form not in kosha/MW as indeclinable

analysis.note: Gerundive/passive verbal adjective from √vac used predicatively with saḥ: ‘to be said/declared’.
Negates the main passive verb dṛśyate; first constituent of na adṛṣṭe > nādṛṣṭe.

Privative past passive participial adjective, in a locative absolute with asmin: ‘when this is unseen/not seen’. The form is also compatible with neuter locative singular, but masculine is chosen to match asmin as referring to the implied masculine svabhāva/term under discussion.

verification.detail: compound stem adṛṣṭa as Pum/Saptami/Eka: pass; relation tatpurusa: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Locative singular pronoun in the locative absolute adṛṣṭe 'smin; the initial a- is elided after final -e and marked by avagraha.
Pronominal subject of dṛśyate, printed sa before the following consonant.

justifications.decision: Construe atas/ataḥ as a causal-deictic indeclinable, with eva intensifying the immediately preceding reason.
options_considered: ‘from this alone’ as a merely spatial ablative phrase
‘therefore’ without the intensifying force of eva

evidence.translation: Grammatically, atas/ataḥ means ‘hence/from this’, and eva strengthens it: ‘just for this reason’.

justifications.decision: Take anyaḥ and ananyaḥ as masculine nominative singular predicate adjectives agreeing with saḥ; paratantrataḥ supplies the ablatival standard ‘from/than the dependent’ for both predicates.
options_considered: paratantrataḥ = ‘because of dependence’ modifying the whole sentence
paratantrataḥ = ablatival ‘from/than the dependent’, governing the difference/non-difference predication
justifications.chosen: saḥ … anyaḥ / ananyaḥ paratantrataḥ = ‘that is neither different nor non-different from the dependent’

evidence.quote: sa na eva anyaḥ na ananyaḥ paratantrataḥ
evidence.translation: The nominative predicate adjectives match saḥ; Sanskrit anya commonly construes with an ablatival expression for ‘different from’, and the same standard is carried over to ananyaḥ.

justifications.decision: Read -vat as the likeness affix, not as possessive -vat; anityatādi supplies an example-class beginning with impermanence.
options_considered: ‘in the manner of impermanence and the like’

evidence.translation: The vati/-vat formation is lexically and grammatically used for resemblance: ‘as/like X’; ādi indicates ‘X and the rest’.

justifications.decision: Analyze vācyaḥ as a gerundive/passive verbal adjective used predicatively with saḥ, rather than as a finite verb.

evidence.translation: The form is masculine nominative singular and agrees with saḥ; gerundive vācya means ‘to be said, to be called, to be declared’.

justifications.decision: Take adṛṣṭe asmin as a locative absolute and take na with the main passive dṛśyate.
options_considered: ‘he does not see in this unseen thing’
‘not when this is unseen, that is seen’
‘when this is unseen, that is not seen’
justifications.chosen: na adṛṣṭe asmin sa dṛśyate = ‘when this is not seen, that is not seen’

evidence.translation: A locative singular participle plus a locative singular pronoun forms a locative absolute; dṛśyate is a present passive, ‘is seen’, negated by na.

justifications.decision: Use the deictic contrast: asmin points to the nearer dependent term signaled by paratantrataḥ, while saḥ continues the nominative subject of the verse.
options_considered: asmin and saḥ both refer to the same entity
asmin refers to the dependent term and saḥ to the other term
both pronouns have indeterminate reference within the verse alone
justifications.chosen: asmin refers to ‘this [dependent]’ and saḥ to the other term under discussion

evidence.translation: The nearest explicit comparative standard is paratantra; the locative asmin naturally resumes it as ‘this’, while saḥ remains the entity being predicated and seen/not seen.

translation:
For that very reason, that is to be declared
neither different nor non-different from the dependent (paratantra),
like impermanence and the like;
when this [dependent] is not seen, that is not seen.
one_shot_delta: A commentary-blind or overly mechanical rendering could take paratantrataḥ as ‘because of dependence’ rather than ‘from/than the dependent’, obscuring the different/non-different construction.
It could read anityatādivat as possessive, ‘having impermanence and so on’, instead of the likeness expression ‘like impermanence and so on’.
It could attach na to the locative phrase nādṛṣṭe rather than to the main verb dṛśyate; the better grammatical construal is ‘when this is unseen, that is not seen’.
It could translate dṛśyate as active/middle ‘sees’ rather than passive ‘is seen’.

Without commentary, the precise antecedents of saḥ and asmin remain partly inferential, so naming the first as a specific Yogācāra technical nature would go beyond the evidence supplied in this ablation run.
analyzer_disagreements: The analyzer’s api_seg offers paratantrāt, but the printed bhāṣya reading is paratantrataḥ; I analyze it as an ablatival tasil-type indeclinable from paratantra, not as the regular ablative singular paratantrāt.
The analyzer treats paratantrataḥ as if it were a case-form of paratantra; grammatically the -taḥ ending here is better taken as an indeclinable ablatival formation meaning ‘from/than the dependent’.
The analyzer splits anitya_tā_ādi_vat; I treat anityatā as one derived abstract stem within anityatādi, with vati/-vat expressing likeness: ‘like impermanence and so on’.
The analyzer splits a separately in na_a_dṛṣṭe; I analyze adṛṣṭe as one privative participial adjective, a nañ-tatpuruṣa/PPP form, in a locative absolute with asmin.
The analyzer lists vācyo under the verbal root vac; for this apparatus it is a subanta gerundive stem vācya, masculine nominative singular, though derived from √vac.
open_questions: No commentary is available in this run, so the precise doctrinal antecedent of saḥ is not fixed here beyond ‘that [term/nature]’.
The items included under ādi in anityatādi cannot be specified from the verse grammar alone.

The locative adṛṣṭe/asmin is formally compatible with masculine or neuter singular; masculine has been chosen for agreement with the implied masculine term, but this remains context-dependent without commentary.
